title: DJI Osmo Pocket 4P Unveiled A Dual-Camera Cinema Tool in Your Pocket
deck: DJI has officially pulled the curtain back on the highly anticipated Osmo Pocket 4P, choosing the prestigious Cannes Film Festival as the stage for its big reveal. While leaks and rumors had already given much of the design away, this announcement marks a clear turning point for the Pocket series — one that pushes it from a creator-focused gimbal camera into something DJI is openly calling a "cinematic imaging device."
imageAlt: Image 2: A promotional graphic for the DJI Osmo Pocket 4P preview event at Cannes, featuring a camera on a tripod at sunset with text: "VISION UNVEILED: An Exclusive Osmo Pocket 4P Preview at Cannes.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Insta360 Go 3S Retro Bundle A Tiny 4K Action Camera Gets a Film Camera Makeover
deck: Insta360 has taken its ultra-compact Go 3S action camera in a completely unexpected direction. Instead of chasing faster frame rates or more rugged specs, the company is embracing nostalgia with the new Go 3S Retro Bundle — a special edition that transforms the wearable 4K POV camera into something that looks and feels like a vintage point-and-shoot from the film era.
imageAlt: Image 1: A close-up of an Insta360 camera is shown on the left, with a collage of two smiling people holding the camera and posing for photos on the right. The background is light and minimalistic.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: 5 Best U.S. National Parks for Spring Photography
deck: After a long winter of muted tones and bare branches, spring breathes color back into the landscape. For photographers, it's arguably the most exciting season of the year—rivers swell with snowmelt, wildflowers blanket the valleys, and wildlife becomes more active. But with 63 national parks across the U.S., picking the right destination can be overwhelming.
imageAlt: Photo of a waterfall at Yosemite National Park
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: 5 Best U.S. National Parks for Summer Photography
deck: Summer in America's national parks means long daylight hours, alpine wildflowers, and dramatic light stretching from early morning to late evening. But it also means crowds—unless you know where to go.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: 8 Lightroom Hacks Every Outdoor Photographer Should Know
deck: Returning from a sunrise shoot with a full memory card is always a good feeling—until you sit down and realize you have hundreds of images to cull and edit. The gap between a great capture and a polished photo often comes down to how efficiently you move through Lightroom Classic.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Sigma 300–600mm f4 Review The Boldest Wildlife Lens in Years
deck: Sigma has never been afraid to do things differently, but the new Sigma 300–600mm f/4 DG OS Sports lens takes that reputation to another level. Designed exclusively for Sony E-mount cameras, this super-telephoto zoom delivers a fixed f/4 aperture across the entire range — a feature normally reserved for prime lenses. It is, by any measure, a daring piece of optical engineering. And as early reviews confirm, it actually works brilliantly in the field.
imageAlt: Photo of someone shooting with the Sigma 300-600mm lens
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Atomos Shinobi II Review A Bright, Compact Field Monitor Worth Your Money in 2026
deck: If you’ve been shooting video professionally—or even just getting serious about filmmaking—you’ve likely run into the limitations of your camera’s tiny built-in screen. That’s where an external monitor changes everything. The Atomos Shinobi II is one of the best-value field monitors to hit the market in 2026, offering a super-bright display, built-in camera controls, and a portable form factor that fits seamlessly into any rig.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Fujifilm X100V Review Is This Retro Compact Camera Still Worth It
deck: **Fujifilm X100V Review: Is This Retro Compact Camera Still Worth It?**
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Nikon 35mm f/1.8G ED Lens Review: The Everyday Prime That Punches Above Its Weight
deck: The 35mm focal length has long been a favorite among photographers for its natural, true-to-life perspective and remarkable versatility. Whether you're documenting a wedding, capturing candid family moments, or wandering city streets for some urban photography, a good 35mm prime can feel like the only lens you need. The Nikon 35mm f/1.8G ED makes a compelling case for being exactly that lens — especially when you consider what it delivers for the price.
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Peak Design Capture Clip V3 Review: The Smartest Way to Carry a Camera on the Trail
deck: For anyone who hikes with a camera, the eternal question is where to put it. Most backpacking packs require you to shrug off the load just to grab your gear. Hand-carrying works for half a mile — then the fatigue sets in. Chest harnesses can chafe against a loaded pack. That is exactly the frustration that led traveler Peter Dering to quit his job in 2011, launch a Kickstarter campaign, and create the original Capture Clip — and with it, the entire Peak Design brand. Now in its third generation, the Capture Clip V3 promises to keep your camera within arm's reach, right on your shoulder strap. Here's how it holds up after months of real-world use.
imageAlt: Image 3: peak design capture camera clip v3
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Peak Design Everyday Backpack V2 Review: Still the Best Camera Bag for Daily Carry?
deck: When Peak Design launched the original Everyday Backpack via Kickstarter back in 2015, it quickly became an icon in the photography world. Fast forward a few years, and the California-based company decided it was time for a refresh. As someone who counts the 20L Everyday Backpack among the favorites in my collection of camera backpacks, I had to find out whether the V2 truly improved on an already beloved design — so I bought one and put it through months of real-world use.
imageAlt: Peak Design Everyday Backpack V2 hero shot
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: Sony a6400 Review: The Compact Mirrorless Camera That Packs a Serious Punch
deck: The Sony a6400 has earned its place as one of the most talked-about mirrorless cameras on the market, and for good reason. With its lightning-fast autofocus, compact body, and impressive image quality, it has captured the attention of hobbyists and traveling photographers alike. But how does it actually hold up under real-world shooting conditions?
author: PhotoMorning Staff

title: The IMAX Camera That Shot Nolan's Next Epic Goes on Public Display
author: Lin Chen
deck: A rare public exhibit turns a production tool into a lesson about scale, craft, and why giant-format filmmaking still feels different.

title: Panasonic Lumix L10 Review: Compact, Stylish, and Capable
author: Maya Hart
deck: A small camera with a confident design asks whether everyday photographers still need a dedicated body in the age of excellent phones.

title: Canon EOS R6 V First Impressions: Why Is Canon Crowding the Field?
author: Jon Park
deck: Canon's latest hybrid body looks familiar at first glance, but its positioning says a lot about where enthusiast cameras are heading.

title: Someone Shared a Real Monet Painting as AI and Asked for Critiques
author: Lena Ortiz
deck: A small online experiment became a revealing mirror for how quickly people judge images through the language of generative media.

title: India's Tiger Reserves Are Banning Smartphone Photography
author: Nikhil Rao
deck: Conservation officials are trying to reduce crowd pressure, but the policy raises bigger questions about access, safety, and attention.

title: DJI Has Finally Unveiled the Dual-Camera Osmo Pocket 4P
author: Erin Vale
deck: DJI's pocket camera line gets a dual-camera twist aimed at vloggers, travel shooters, and creators who want a lighter kit.

title: No, the Sony a7R VI Doesn't Make the a1 II Obsolete
author: Chris Nolan
deck: Resolution is seductive, but camera value is still about speed, workflow, handling, and the kind of pressure a body can survive.

title: The Chinese Lens Boom Continues With a Flood of New Releases
author: Hannah Lee
deck: A wave of aggressive lens makers is changing expectations for price, character, autofocus, and how quickly unusual ideas reach market.

title: YouTube Will Help You Find Videos Featuring Your AI Clone
author: Pesala Bandara
deck: Likeness detection is moving from celebrity concern to everyday creator tool as AI-generated video becomes easier to publish.

title: Newspaper Deletes Photo of Unconscious Motorbike Rider After Backlash
author: Matt Growcoot
deck: A sports image prompted a familiar but urgent question: when does news value stop justifying public exposure of a vulnerable person?

title: Exhibition Marks 70 Years Since Gordon Parks' Landmark Photos
author: Avery Brooks
deck: A new exhibition revisits color photographs that made social history feel immediate, intimate, and impossible to ignore.

title: Laowa Launches the World's Widest Probe Zoom Lenses
author: Kate Garibaldi
deck: The specialized macro probe lens gets wider and more flexible, giving tabletop, food, and product filmmakers another strange tool.

title: DIY Project Turns a Panasonic GF3 and Soviet FED 5 Into a Digital Rangefinder
author: Jeremy Gray
deck: A patient camera builder combines old rangefinder charm with cheap digital parts and reminds us why tinkering still matters.

title: Adobe Has Run Out of Allies
author: Sam Keller
deck: Photographers are not merely complaining about subscriptions; they are asking whether their tools still feel like partners.

title: The DaVinci Resolve 21 Photo Editing Tools Show Promise
author: Nora Fields
deck: Blackmagic's editor is still built for motion, but its expanding still-image tools hint at a more unified creative workflow.

title: Gerald Undone Is Done Reviewing Cameras
author: Micah Reed
deck: A beloved reviewer stepping away from camera coverage says as much about the review economy as it does about gear fatigue.

title: Photography Isn't Dying, Says VSCO
author: Iris Young
deck: The craft keeps changing shape, but the hunger to make personal images has not disappeared; it has moved into new rituals.

title: Photographer Leaves Camera Trap in Remote Maasai Mara and Discovers Hidden Wildlife Highway
author: Dana Morris
deck: A patient field project reveals animal movement that would be nearly impossible to witness from a vehicle or walking trail.

title: Photographer's Journey Deep into Canadian Rainforest to Find Rare Spirit Bear
author: Theo Ames
deck: Searching for a rare bear becomes a story about weather, restraint, local knowledge, and the ethics of not forcing the frame.

title: Four Years With Mountain Villagers Forced to Defend Their Livelihood
author: Rina Shah
deck: A long-term documentary project follows families trying to preserve land, work, and identity while outside pressure keeps rising.
